JAISP

A joint Rubin–Euclid foundation model for cross-instrument source detection and astrometry

— and a path to Roman —

Rubin · ugrizy Euclid · VIS YJH Roman · next
Shoubaneh Hemmati · IPAC, Caltech with G. Helou, Y.-H. Lin, R. Akeson, C. D. Dowell, A. Faisst, T. Greene, D. Shupe · Hemmati et al. 2026 · github.com/xoubish/JAISP
Roman Science Conference · Caltech/IPAC · July 2026
Motivation

Why combine surveys at the pixel level?

  • Cosmology is now limited by systematics, not statistics: the remaining disagreements between cosmological probes are too significant to ignore, but we cannot yet tell measurement error apart from new physics.
  • Rubin/LSST, Euclid, and Roman observe overlapping sky from ~0.3 to 2 μm at sub-arcsecond resolution — in combination they can calibrate and cross-check one another, suppressing survey-specific systematics no single survey controls alone.
  • Working at the pixel level means handling two instruments with different resolutions, PSFs, depths, and noise at the same time — something no single-survey pipeline was built to do.
  • The goal is not to discard classical pipelines, but to move the joint, cross-instrument part of the problem into a representation learned from the pixels themselves.
Approach

Train one model once — reuse it for every measurement

  • The model trains itself: hide one of the ten bands and ask it to predict that band's image from the other nine. The images are their own supervision — no labels, no catalogs, no simulations.
  • Then lock it and reuse it: every measurement afterwards — detection, positions, and more — is a small add-on network reading the same stored features, at a tiny fraction of the training cost.
  • Detection and astrometry are working today; photometry and shape measurement are in preparation — all reading the same features.

Cost structure

2.5 GPU-hourstotal pretraining cost
Model size~9M parameters
Featurescomputed once per dataset
Each new taska small add-on network
Each new fieldzero retraining (→ slide 13)
Data

The earliest public release of each survey

ECDFS training field · held-out EDF-S · the ten-band stack of one matched tile
  • Rubin DP1 (commissioning camera, ugrizy, 0.2″/px, ~1″ seeing) + Euclid Q1 (VIS and near-infrared YJH, 0.1″/px): 790 overlapping ten-band image tiles covering ~0.2 deg² of one deep field — the first public release of each survey.
  • A second deep field (EDF-S) is set aside untouched — the model never sees it — to test at the end whether everything transfers to new sky.
Architecture

Each instrument at its native resolution

each band through its own small network → one branch per instrument → merged ten-band layer → measurement modules
  • Rubin stays at 0.2″/px, Euclid at 0.1″/px — no resampling onto a shared grid, so neither instrument's spatial information is thrown away. The branches merge on a common 0.4″ grid, where all ten bands exchange information.
Architecture

We didn't start here: ten iterations to this design

v1–v2 Contrastive patch matching — make matching Rubin/Euclid cutouts look similar in feature space failed · sky-dominated
v3 Object-centric matching — discover individual sources, pair them across instruments abandoned · no precision
v4–v5 Feature matching on full tiles (JEPA) — make the two instruments' features agree at corresponding sky positions, with ever-stricter constraints beaten by a simple CNN
v6 The turn — predict actual pixels — hide one band, reconstruct its image: no shortcut that discards position paradigm shift
v7–v8 Mixed resolution — one branch per instrument at its native pixel scale, merged at a common physical scale works
v9–v10 Refinements — every band gets an equal vote during training; the loss focuses on real sources, especially their cores production
Features that look similar aren't features that know where — the year of negative results is the design.
Validation · 1 of 2

Did it learn cross-instrument structure?

truth · reconstruction · residual — held-out tiles
  • Withhold one band on held-out tiles; predict it from the other nine.
  • Rubin r, Euclid VIS, and NISP H all reconstructed faithfully — residuals confined to bright cores and faint galaxy wings.
  • A band from one instrument, predicted from bands of the other: genuine cross-instrument correspondence, not within-instrument copying.
Validation · 2 of 2

…and is it organized physically?

decodability of the frozen features · information routing between bands
  • A simple linear fit reads each band's brightness straight back out of the features (R² ≈ 0.7–0.95) — the information is there and easy to reach. And the model leans on bands the way real galaxy colors would predict: VIS is the sharp scaffold everything is built on; Rubin bands draw on their spectral neighbors; the three near-infrared bands mostly predict one another.
Task 1 · Detection

A detection head on frozen features

held-out VIS tile · four source lists overlaid
  • A small detection network turns the ten-band features into a source-evidence map — finding sources is still peak-finding, as in classical detection, but on all ten bands at once.
  • Trained on lightweight classical labels (standard 3σ source extraction on VIS) — no curated catalog needed.
  • Matches the Euclid Q1 MER catalog on held-out sky, and returns one detection per galaxy where classical extraction fragments extended systems.
Task 1 · Detection

Ten bands buy real depth

injection completeness · MER recovery · working point
+0.45 magdeeper from ten-band fusion · 50% depth VIS ≈ 25.0
50% depth (VIS mag)ten-band featuresper-band control
all 10 bands25.025.1
VIS only24.625.1
multi-band gain+0.45+0.03
  • Depth is measured by hiding real sources, dimmed to a chosen magnitude, in the images and counting how many are found — no reference catalog needed.
  • Against the Euclid catalog: 93% completeness, 95% purity (an undercount — some “false” detections are real sources the VIS-selected catalog missed).
  • An identical detector reading per-band features gains only +0.03 mag: the depth comes from the combined ten-band representation itself.
Task 2 · Astrometry

Carrying VIS precision to every band

before correction (dashed) vs after (solid) · position offsets between the two surveys
  • The same sources measured in both surveys disagree by ~50 mas — mostly photon noise in centroiding faint galaxies. A small correction network reads the model's features at each source and shrinks this to 14–17 mas in every band.
  • Is it real? We hide sources at known positions in the images: corrected positions err by only 19 mas at S/N = 5 (classical: ~50 mas) — nearly the ~17 mas precision of the sharp VIS channel itself, now carried into every band.
Task 2 · Astrometry

The concordance field: how well do two Gaia-tied surveys agree?

two independent fits of the pattern · after per-source correction · shared vs per-band parts
  • Beneath the per-source scatter sits a coherent 9–10 mas pattern between the two surveys' astrometric solutions — even though both are tied to Gaia. About 7 mas is shared by all ten bands: one cross-survey systematic.
  • Two entirely different fitting methods recover the same pattern, so it is not an artifact of either; the per-source correction absorbs it (→ 1.5 mas).
  • The result is a few-milliarcsecond map of how well two independently calibrated surveys actually agree — quality assurance no single pipeline can produce.
Transfer

A second deep field — nothing retrained

EDF-S, 72 tiles never seen by foundation or heads
  • On the never-seen field, detection reaches 93.4% completeness and 93.1% purity — vs 93.3% / 94.5% on the training field — and positions land on the same 12–18 mas floor. Covering more sky means applying the model, not rebuilding it.
Roman

Roman: the natural third stream

Why the model is ready

  • One encoder branch per instrument is the design — adding Roman means adding a third branch, not redesigning the model.
  • Training needs only overlapping public images — no labels, no simulations — and hours of GPU time, once WFI imaging overlaps Rubin/Euclid sky.

What Roman adds

  • Sharp, deep near-infrared imaging at 0.11″/px — the infrared counterpart of the sharp VIS reference, where today we must rely on resampled NISP.
  • A wide survey of thousands of deg² inside the LSST footprint, plus deep time-domain fields.

What Roman gains

  • Detection that uses all surveys at once — for Rubin+Euclid that was worth +0.45 mag — and Roman-quality positions carried into the ground-based bands.
  • Three-way agreement maps — Rubin–Euclid–Roman astrometry compared at the few-mas level — and cross-instrument alignment solved inside the model instead of inherited from preprocessing (the ~3 mas NISP resampling error we found is exactly that).
The launch window opens August 30 — the recipe is ready.
Takeaways

One representation — tasks, fields, instruments

  • A small (~9M-parameter) foundation model, trained without any labels on public Rubin + Euclid images in ~2.5 GPU-hours, learns a shared ten-band representation precise enough to measure with.
  • Detection: matches the Euclid catalog (93% / 95%), and ten-band fusion pushes 0.45 mag deeper than VIS alone.
  • Astrometry: VIS-quality positions in every band — ~50 → 14–17 mas, verified at 19 mas on hidden test sources — plus a few-mas map of the disagreement between the two surveys' Gaia-tied solutions.
  • The whole stack transfers unchanged to a second deep field — all on the earliest data both surveys have released: a floor, not a ceiling.
  • Roman slots in as a third encoder stream: a sharp NIR anchor, and three-way cross-survey calibration.
Hemmati et al. 2026 · github.com/xoubish/JAISP · shemmati@caltech.edu
